h1: Georgia Disability Property Tax Exemption Appeal Letter Generator
metaTitle: Georgia Disability Tax Exemption Appeal Demand Letter
metaDescription: Generate a Georgia disability property tax exemption appeal demand letter. State-specific deadlines, statutes, and proven strategy to challenge denials fast.
deadline: 45 days from the date of the assessment notice or denial
penalty: Reversal of denial, refund of overpaid taxes, and potential interest under O.C.G.A. § 48-5-380
introParagraph: If you are a disabled Georgia homeowner, you may qualify for significant property tax relief under state law. Disabled veterans, totally disabled residents, and certain surviving spouses can claim exemptions worth tens of thousands of dollars in assessed value. But county tax assessors and Boards of Assessors regularly deny these exemptions due to paperwork issues, missed deadlines, or misapplication of disability rating rules. Georgia law gives you a strict 45-day window to appeal a denial or assessment, and a well-drafted demand letter can resolve the dispute before you ever see a courtroom. This page explains how Georgia's disability exemption laws work, what your appeal letter must contain, and how to preserve your rights to escalate to the Board of Equalization or Superior Court.
lawExplanation:
Georgia provides several disability-based property tax exemptions, each with distinct eligibility rules. Under O.C.G.A. § 48-5-48, a disabled veteran with a 100% service-connected disability rating from the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs (or who is rated as entitled to receive 100% compensation due to unemployability) qualifies for an exemption from ad valorem taxes on a homestead. The exemption amount is adjusted annually and currently exceeds $100,000 in assessed value. Surviving unremarried spouses and minor children of qualifying veterans may also claim the exemption.

O.C.G.A. § 48-5-52 provides a separate homestead exemption for residents age 62 or older who are totally disabled, subject to income limits. Many counties also offer local disability homestead exemptions through local legislation that exceed the state baseline.

To qualify, applicants must file with the county tax commissioner or tax assessor by April 1 of the tax year, providing proof of disability such as a VA award letter, Social Security determination, or two physicians' affidavits where required. The Board of Tax Assessors reviews the application and issues a written decision.

If the exemption is denied or the property is improperly assessed, O.C.G.A. § 48-5-311 governs the appeal. Taxpayers have 45 days from the date of the notice to file a written appeal. Appeals can be directed to the County Board of Equalization, a hearing officer (for non-homestead property over $750,000), or to binding arbitration. Decisions of the Board of Equalization may be further appealed to Superior Court within 30 days. Georgia law requires assessors to consider all evidence submitted, and the burden shifts to the county to justify denial when the taxpayer presents substantiating disability documentation.
letterStrategy:
A demand letter in a Georgia disability exemption dispute serves three goals: it creates a documented record, triggers statutory review obligations, and often produces a quick reversal without the cost of formal proceedings. The letter should be addressed to the County Board of Tax Assessors and copied to the Tax Commissioner, citing O.C.G.A. § 48-5-48 (or the applicable subsection) and O.C.G.A. § 48-5-311.

Open by identifying the property by parcel ID, the tax year at issue, and the date of the denial or assessment notice. State the exemption claimed and attach all supporting documentation: VA disability rating letter, Social Security disability determination, physician affidavits, proof of age, deed showing homestead ownership, and prior year's tax bill if relevant. Explicitly demand that the Board reverse the denial, recalculate the assessment, and issue a corrected tax bill or refund.

Include a deadline for response, typically 14 to 21 days, and reference the 45-day appeal clock to make clear you intend to preserve your rights before the Board of Equalization if the matter is not resolved. Many Georgia county boards will reopen and correct denials administratively when presented with complete documentation in a formal letter, particularly when the original denial was based on incomplete forms rather than substantive ineligibility. If the demand is ignored, the letter becomes Exhibit A in your formal appeal and demonstrates good-faith effort to resolve the dispute.
proceduralNotes: Georgia appeals begin with the County Board of Equalization at no cost to the taxpayer. If you appeal to Superior Court, filing fees range from approximately $200 to $230 depending on the county, and the county must pay costs if the final value is 80% or less of the Board's decision. Small claims court in Georgia (Magistrate Court) has a $15,000 jurisdictional limit and can hear refund disputes but not exemption denials, which must follow the § 48-5-311 process. The 45-day appeal deadline is jurisdictional and cannot be extended. Counties must mail assessment notices annually. Keep the certified mail receipt from your appeal letter as proof of timely filing.

faq.q: Who qualifies for Georgia's disabled veteran property tax exemption?
faq.a: Veterans with a 100% service-connected disability rating from the VA, or those rated as totally and permanently disabled due to unemployability, qualify under O.C.G.A. § 48-5-48. The exemption also extends to unremarried surviving spouses and minor children. You must own and occupy the home as your primary residence and file an application with the county tax commissioner, including a VA award letter as proof. The exemption amount is set by federal formula and adjusts annually.

faq.q: How long do I have to appeal a denied disability exemption in Georgia?
faq.a: You have 45 days from the date printed on the denial notice or annual assessment notice to file a written appeal under O.C.G.A. § 48-5-311. This deadline is strict and cannot be extended for any reason, including illness or mail delays. File your appeal with the Board of Tax Assessors by certified mail or in person, and keep proof of the filing date. Missing this window forfeits your appeal rights for that tax year.

faq.q: Can I appeal to small claims court in Georgia?
faq.a: No. Georgia property tax exemption disputes must follow the statutory appeal process under O.C.G.A. § 48-5-311, beginning with the County Board of Equalization, a hearing officer, or arbitration. Magistrate Court (small claims) has a $15,000 limit but lacks jurisdiction over exemption denials and assessment disputes. After exhausting the administrative process, you may appeal to Superior Court within 30 days of the Board of Equalization's decision.

faq.q: What documents should I include with my appeal letter?
faq.a: Include your VA disability rating letter or Social Security disability award, two physician affidavits if claiming total disability under O.C.G.A. § 48-5-52, the deed to your home, your driver's license showing the property as your address, the denial notice or assessment notice you received, and prior year's tax bill if relevant. Reference each document by exhibit number in the letter and send everything by certified mail with return receipt requested.

faq.q: Will I have to pay taxes while my appeal is pending?
faq.a: Yes. Under Georgia law, you must pay the temporary tax bill (typically 85% of the prior year's taxes or the current assessment, whichever applies) while the appeal is pending. If you win, the county refunds the overpayment with interest under O.C.G.A. § 48-5-380. Failing to pay the temporary bill can result in liens and interest charges. Keep payment records to support any refund claim once the exemption is granted.
